Application of AI using Unstructured Data

Artificial Intelligence for Customer Service Subfields of Customer Service using AI

Source: Textbook - Applied Artificial Intelligence in Business: Concepts and Cases https://primoa.library.unsw.edu.au/permalink/f/jhud33/UNSW_ALMA51331715730001731  Artificial Intelligence In Customer Service • Sentiment Analysis of Social Media Data • Unstructured data: Customer complaints, opinions, feelings

• Product Reviews Analysis on Amazon • Unstructured data reflecting genuine customer experiences/ opinions • Extract key topics discussed in the reviews • Identify frequent complaints, issues and pain points

Artificial Intelligence In Customer Service • Facial recognition/Image analysis in retail store • Audio analysis for customer support call recordings

• Voice assistants and speech analysis • Call Center recordings, voicemail messages • Identify the emotional tone of the speech • Provide instant responses to customer queries, order tracking, FAQs

Why Feature Extraction Matters? Transforming Raw Data • Problem: Diverse data formats Images, videos, text, audio • Solution: Turn unstructured data into structured data Suitable to be fed into ML algorithms

Reducing Dimensionality • Problem We will use PCA in • High dimensionality of unstructured data (too many variables) Hand-On Exercise 2 • Noise and irrelevant information • Solution • Reduce the number of variables by identifying the most relevant features • Example: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)  Feature Extraction for Unstructured Data Transforming Text Data Example of Named Entity Recognition • N-grams • Named Entity Recognition (NER) • Sentiment Analysis

Source: https://www.shaip.com/blog/named-entity-recognition-and-its-types/

Feature Extraction for Unstructured Data Transforming Image Data We will use VGG16 in • Edge Detection Hand-On Exercise 2

• Feature mapping using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) Using the VGG16 CNN for feature extraction

Source: http://dx.doi.org/10.1109/ACCESS.2019.2935222   Hand-On Exercise 1

Inference using a pre-trained model in or

Objective Let’s use the pre-trained AI model we generated in week 1 (from Teachable Machine) in Python or R • Dataset • Sample images from the Fruits 360 dataset of 90,380 images of 131 fruits and vegetables are made available on Moodle for download (under Week 1 section) • Model type: Convolutional Neural Network Please use your preferred language! • Environment or

How to use Python / R in Google Colab? • In Google Colab, you can create Python or R notebooks to run analytics • Notebooks can be created from scratch or uploaded as .ipynb files • Two notebook files are available on Moodle • Week4_Hands_On_Exercise1_Python.ipynb • Week4_Hands_On_Exercise1_R.ipynb • They contain Python and R code respectively for this Hands-On exercise

# Load the libraries needed, including numpy, tensorflow # Install the keras library and keras install.packages("keras") import numpy as np import tensorflow as tf # Load the keras library from tensorflow import keras library(keras) from PIL import Image, ImageOps #Install pillow instead of PIL # Load your pre-trained model trained during week 1 along with the labels # Load your pre-trained model trained during week 1 along model = load_model_hdf5("/content/keras_model.h5") with the labels labels = as.vector(read.delim("/content/labels.txt", model = header=FALSE)$V1) keras.models.load_model("/content/keras_model.h5", compile=False) # Load one image to classify and resise it to be at least class_names = open('/content/labels.txt', 224x224 'r').readlines() img_path = "/content/pomegrenate.jpg" img <- image_load(img_path, target_size = c(224,224)) # Load one image to classify and resise it to be at least 224x224 image = Image.open('/content/pomegrenate.jpg').convert('RGB') image = ImageOps.fit(image, (224, 224), Image.BICUBIC)

# Pre-process the image including: # Pre-process the image including: # Turn the image into a numpy array # Turn the image into a numpy array image_array = np.asarray(image) img_tensor <- image_to_array(img) # Normalize the image # Normalize the image img_tensor <- (img_tensor / 127.0) - 1 normalized_image_array = (image_array.astype(np.float32) # Create the array of the right shape to feed into the / 127.0) - 1 keras model img_tensor <- array_reshape(img_tensor, c(1, 224, 224, # Create the array of the right shape to feed into the 3)) keras model data = np.ndarray(shape=(1, 224, 224, 3), # Make predictions then display the label with the dtype=np.float32) highest prediction probability # Load the image into the array preds = model %>% predict(img_tensor) data[0] = normalized_image_array cat("Class: ", labels[[which.max(preds)]], "\n") cat("Confidence score: ", preds[[which.max(preds)]], # Make predictions then display the label with the "\n") highest prediction probability prediction = model.predict(data) index = np.argmax(prediction) class_name = class_names[index] confidence_score = prediction[0][index]

print('Class:', class_name, end='') print('Confidence score:', confidence_score)

Deep Learning for Unstructured Data Analysis

Deep Neural Networks

• Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) What are CNNs? • Automatically and adaptively learn spatial hierarchies of features from input images • Image recognition • Video analysis • Medical imaging diagnostics

• Recurrent Neural Networks / Long Short-Term Memory Networks What are RNNs/LSTMs? • Suited for sequential data like text and speech • Natural Language Processing (NLP): Tasks such as language modelling, translation • Speech Recognition: Convert spoken language into text • Text Generation: Create new text based on input sequences

• Transformers What are Transformers? • Process input data in parallel, which significantly accelerates and optimises the training process: Faster and more efficient training! Popular Models: • BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) • GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) • NLP Tasks: Language understanding, question answering, text generation

• Autoencoders • Neural networks designed to copy their inputs to their outputs Applications • Feature learning: Extract meaningful features from raw data • Anomaly detection: Identify unusual patterns or outliers • Noise reduction: Clean up data by removing noise • Variational autoencoders (VAEs) • Used for generating complex data like images and music

• Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) • Consist of two networks: a generator and a discriminator • How they work? Generator: Creates new data samples Discriminator: Evaluates the authenticity of the generated samples • Enhancing and Generating Art: Create new artworks/ improve existing ones • Generating Realistic Images: Produce lifelike images from random noise • Video Generation and Editing: Generate and edit video content

• Content recommendation • Custom experiences • Type of ML models • Deep learning • Generative AI

1 Identify the Business Problem

• Content Generation: Producing articles, music, videos, and games at scale • Tailored Content Recommendations to match user preferences • Immersive Media: Creating engaging and interactive media experiences • Efficiency and Cost Reduction: Streamlining content creation • To save time and reduce production costs • Engagement and Retention: Boosting viewer or user engagement and retention rates with immersive content

2 Translate the Business Problem into a Machine Learning Problem

• Natural Language Processing and Computer Vision: For content generation, • NLP to create textual content • Computer vision for images and videos • Recommendation Systems: Building ML models that learn users' preferences/behaviours to personalise content recommendations • Automated Content Creation: Generative models like GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks) to create realistic images, videos, and music

3 Prepare Relevant Data (Collect, Clean, Transform)

• Data Collection: Gather diverse datasets from content libraries, user interactions, and feedback • Data Cleaning: Remove irrelevant, duplicate, or poor-quality data • Enhance ML model performance Data Transformation: Convert data into a suitable format for ML models e.g., tokenize text or extract features from images/videos

4 Analyse the Data (Training, Testing)

• Model Training • Use prepared data to train generative AI models • Adjust parameters to enhance creativity and relevance of content • Model Testing and Evaluation • Test the models against a separate dataset • Evaluate performance, using metrics like accuracy, novelty, and user engagement

6 Generate Actionable Insights from the Key Findings

• Enhanced Personalisation: Highly personalised media experiences • Watching movies, listening to music or podcasts, reading books • To drive engagement, satisfaction, and loyalty • Innovative Products and Services: Create new forms of entertainment and interactive experiences, • e.g. AI-generated music, VR environment, or personalised storytelling • Operational Efficiency: Automate content and production tasks • Creators can focus on strategic and creative work!

Hand-On Deep Dive

Let’s build an image classifier in Python or R

Let’s build an image classifier in Python or R

Objective Train a model in Python or R to classify images into different categories • Dataset o Training.zip is a zip file containing 600 images (300 cheese and 300 pepperoni) to be used for training the model o Testing.zip is a zip file containing 200 images (100 cheese and 100 pepperoni) to be used for testing the model Each zip file contains two subfolders, one for images of plain cheese pizza, and one for images of pepperoni pizza). The images are a subset of an annotated multi-ingredient pizza image dataset called Pizza10 https://arxiv.org/abs/2012.02821  How to use Python / R in Google Colab?

In Google Colab, you can create Python or R notebooks to run analytics Notebooks can be created from scratch or uploaded as .ipynb files Two notebook files are available on Moodle • Week4_Hands_On_Deep_Dive_Python.ipynb • Week4_Hands_On_Deep_Dive_R.ipynb

✓ They contain Python and R code for this Hands-On Deep Dive
